title: The Dark Corridors of Blackmoor
subtitle: London, 2026
description: At Blackmoor Asylum, London's oldest psychiatric hospital, director Dr. Richard Pemberton was found dead in his office. The official report says heart attack, but the autopsy tells a different story: lethal drug poisoning. 6 suspects, 6 broken alibis, 6 dark secrets. Everyone is lying, but only one is the killer. Can you find the truth within the walls of Blackmoor, Detective?

characters.name: Dr. Evelyn Marsh
characters.relationToVictim: The victim's deputy and chief psychiatrist, authorized to prescribe medication
characters.alibi: I was in my office at the time of the incident, writing patient reports. My computer logs confirm this.

characters.name: Dr. Marcus Whitfield
characters.relationToVictim: Runs an experimental treatment program at the hospital, had budget disputes with Pemberton
characters.alibi: I was in the research laboratory, working on experiment data. I was alone.

characters.name: Rahibe Agnes Blackwood
characters.relationToVictim: Has been at the hospital for 30 years, provides spiritual support to patients, has an old dispute with Pemberton
characters.alibi: I was praying in the chapel. Same time every evening. God is my witness.

characters.name: Thomas Hargrove
characters.relationToVictim: Responsible for the hospital grounds and building maintenance, has access to everywhere
characters.alibi: I was in the garden, checking on the greenhouse. It was raining but I was inside the greenhouse.

characters.name: Catherine Vale
characters.relationToVictim: Night shift nurse, responsible for medication distribution, working on a secret project with Pemberton
characters.alibi: I was distributing medication. It's written in the logbook. But I couldn't keep records for 20 minutes due to a patient's crisis.

characters.name: Victor Ashbourne
characters.relationToVictim: Has been receiving treatment at Blackmoor for 2 years, Pemberton's most important case
characters.alibi: I was in my cell. Where would I go? My door is locked, I have an ankle monitor.

evidence.title: Autopsy Report
evidence.description: Dr. Richard Pemberton's forensic report. Although official records state heart attack, the autopsy results tell a different story: 800mg of Thorazine was detected in the blood. This dose is definitely lethal, and it was determined that the drug was mixed into his tea.

evidence.title: Pharmacology Report
evidence.description: The hospital pharmacy's pharmacology analysis report. Evelyn's prescription dosages are borderline. On the night of the incident at 20:45, Thorazine was withdrawn from the pharmacy using Evelyn's password. However, the password may have been shared with others.

evidence.title: Agnes's Secret Keys
evidence.description: A key ring found in Sister Agnes's chapel cabinet. Keys to all critical doors in the hospital are present -- offices, pharmacy, basement. Pemberton's name is written in fresh ink on her prayer list.

evidence.title: Agnes's Letter
evidence.description: A letter from 1994 that Agnes had been hiding. It contains details about the death of 14-year-old patient Timothy Walsh. Pemberton, who was a young doctor at the time, was held responsible. Agnes had been keeping this secret for 30 years.

evidence.title: Pharmacy Access Log
evidence.description: The hospital pharmacy's stock and access records. 800mg of Thorazine is missing. Four people have access to the pharmacy: Evelyn, Marcus, Catherine, and Thomas. However, the card system is outdated and can be cloned.

evidence.title: Evelyn's Prescription Records
evidence.description: Dr. Evelyn Marsh's prescription history for the last 6 months. She prescribed a total of 2400mg excess Thorazine to patients. An accumulation that far exceeds the lethal dose of 800mg. Pemberton had discovered this and started questioning Evelyn.

evidence.title: Medication Distribution Records
evidence.description: Catherine Vale's medication distribution logbook. There is an unexplained 20-minute gap between 21:10 and 21:30. Catherine explains this with Patient #47 having a seizure, but she is the only witness. The gap coincides exactly with the time of death.

evidence.title: Marcus's Research File
evidence.description: Dr. Marcus Whitfield's secret research data. He conducted unethical experiments on 12 patients, 3 of whom experienced serious side effects. Pemberton knew about this and was threatening to expose him. There is a back staircase connecting the laboratory to the pharmacy.

evidence.title: Marcus's Corridor Sighting
evidence.description: Nurse Janet Powell's testimony. She saw Marcus in the West Wing corridor at 21:20. He was in a hurry and slowed down in front of Pemberton's office. Marcus had claimed he was in his laboratory at that time.

evidence.title: Pemberton's Tea Cup
evidence.description: Analysis results of the tea cup in Pemberton's office. The drug was placed directly in the cup, not the thermos. Gloves were used, no fingerprints. The killer must have entered the office between 21:00 and 21:30.

evidence.title: Pemberton's Diary
evidence.description: Pemberton's personal diary found in his locked drawer. Five different suspicious matters are noted: Evelyn's excessive dosages, Marcus's unethical research, Agnes's 1994 secret, unauthorized entry to the office, and secret meetings. Everyone has something to hide.

evidence.title: Cleaning Staff Log
evidence.description: The hospital cleaning crew's night shift records. A critical finding that debunks Evelyn's alibi: the restroom she claimed to have used was closed for maintenance at that time. Evelyn changed her explanation, saying she went to the archive room.

evidence.title: Thomas's Glove
evidence.description: Laboratory analysis of Thomas Hargrove's garden glove. Thorazine residue was detected on the inner surface. However, anyone can take gloves from the storage. This contamination, which cannot be explained by external contact, indicates that the person wearing the glove had direct contact with the drug.

evidence.title: Thomas's Sister
evidence.description: It was revealed that Thomas's sister Emily died during her treatment at Blackmoor. Thomas holds Pemberton responsible for Emily's death. However, rather than seeking revenge, he was collecting documents to expose the truth through legal channels.

evidence.title: Victor's Monitor Record
evidence.description: Data record from Victor Ashbourne's ankle monitor. There was a 15-minute signal loss between 21:20 and 21:35 -- a situation that had never occurred before. If the monitor is removed and reattached within 30 seconds, the alarm system does not trigger.

evidence.title: Security Camera Footage
evidence.description: Blackmoor Hospital security camera footage. Movements during the critical time period: Evelyn missing for 4 minutes between 21:15-21:19, Catherine passes with medication cart at 21:25, Thomas goes down to the basement at 21:32, Agnes only appears at 22:05.

evidence.title: Carpet Fiber
evidence.description: A fiber sample taken from the carpet in Pemberton's office. The same fiber was found on Evelyn's clothing. Evelyn tried to normalize this, but there is no entry record for her in the office on the day of the incident. During interrogation, she lost her composure and shouted 'it's a setup.'

evidence.title: Chapel Door Record
evidence.description: Chapel entry-exit records. Agnes said she was praying in the chapel, but she was seen in the cafeteria at 20:30. Her whereabouts between 20:30 and 22:05 are not recorded.

scenes.text:
London. Midnight. The rain won't stop.

Blackmoor Asylum, a Gothic structure from the Victorian era on the north side of the city. It has housed the mentally ill for 150 years. A terrifying place with its dark corridors, towers with broken windows, and centuries-old garden.

Last night, hospital director Dr. Richard Pemberton was found dead in his office. The official report says heart attack, but you're here because Scotland Yard thinks otherwise.

Inspector Collins meets you at the door: 'The autopsy results are in. Not a heart attack -- drug poisoning. A lethal dose of Thorazine. A drug that everyone in this hospital has access to.'

The rain intensifies. Behind the dark walls of Blackmoor, each of the 6 suspects is lying.

choices.text: Examine the crime scene - Pemberton's office
Examine the autopsy report in detail
Get a briefing from Inspector Collins

scenes.text:
Pemberton's office is large, filled with dark wooden furniture. Diplomas and old patient photographs line the walls. On the desk, a tea thermos and a cup.

Police tape everywhere. The forensics team has finished their work.

The tea cup on the desk has a brown residue. The thermos is clean but the cup is not. Someone added something to the cup.

In the desk drawer, there is a locked diary. The lock has been broken, opened by police. The last entries are striking.

The antique carpet in front of the window is worn but well-maintained. Dark burgundy, thickly woven. It stretches from the door to the desk.
scenes.addCharacterNote: Suspicious residue in the tea cup in Pemberton's office. Locked diary on the desk. Dark burgundy antique carpet on the floor.

scenes.text:
The autopsy report is in your hands. Forensic specialist Dr. Sarah Webb presents the findings:

'We detected 8 times the normal dose of Thorazine in the blood. 800 milligrams. That dose would take down an elephant.'

Report details:
- The drug was taken orally
- Earl Grey tea residue in stomach contents
- Time of death: between 21:30 and 22:00
- Pemberton's known heart condition accelerated the drug's effect

Dr. Webb adds: 'This cannot be an accident. Nobody takes 800mg of Thorazine by mistake. This is premeditated murder.'

Thorazine is a commonly used drug in psychiatric hospitals. Blackmoor's pharmacy has sufficient stock.
scenes.addCharacterNote: Pemberton was poisoned with 800mg Thorazine. The drug was ingested with the first sip of tea. Premeditated murder.

scenes.text:
You enter the hospital security room. Old monitors, a system that transitioned from VHS to digital. Security guard Dave greets you.

'I pulled up the West Wing corridor camera, Detective.'

You watch the recordings:

21:15 - Dr. Evelyn Marsh leaves her office, heads toward the end of the corridor (restroom direction)
21:19 - Evelyn returns, enters her office
21:25 - Catherine Vale passes through the corridor with the medication cart
21:32 - Thomas Hargrove goes down the stairs (toward the basement)
21:45 - Corridor is empty
22:05 - Agnes comes from the chapel direction, knocks on Pemberton's door
22:10 - Agnes is in a panic, calls security

Evelyn's 4-minute absence is notable. The restroom is near Pemberton's office.

choices.text: Why did Thomas go down to the basement?
Why did Agnes knock on Pemberton's door?
Run a technical analysis on Victor's monitor data

scenes.addCharacterNote: Camera footage: Evelyn missing for 4 min (21:15-21:19), Catherine passed with medication cart (21:25), Thomas went to basement (21:32), Agnes arrived at 22:05.

scenes.text:
Inspector Collins gives the briefing:

'There are 6 suspects at Blackmoor. Each of them had issues with Pemberton, and all of them have been confirmed to be in the hospital that night.'

1. Dr. Evelyn Marsh (48) - Chief Psychiatrist. Pemberton was investigating her prescription records.
2. Dr. Marcus Whitfield (57) - Research Director. Pemberton discovered his secret experiments.
3. Sister Agnes Blackwood (63) - Hospital Chaplain. Hiding a 30-year-old secret.
4. Thomas Hargrove (43) - Gardener. His sister died in this hospital.
5. Catherine Vale (32) - Head Nurse. Was working on a secret project with Pemberton.
6. Victor Ashbourne (37) - Patient. Former university professor, in treatment for 2 years.

Collins adds: 'Everyone has an alibi, but I'm not convinced by any of them. Everyone in this hospital is lying, Detective.'

choices.text: Start talking to Dr. Evelyn Marsh

scenes.addCharacterNote: 6 suspects: Evelyn (chief psychiatrist), Marcus (research dir.), Agnes (chaplain), Thomas (gardener), Catherine (nurse), Victor (patient). Everyone has a motive.

scenes.text:
Results have come in from the forensics laboratory.

Tea cup analysis:
- High concentration of Thorazine residue
- Only fingerprints: Pemberton's own
- The person who placed the drug either used gloves or didn't touch the cup

Thermos analysis:
- Clean, no drugs
- The drug was placed directly in the cup, not the thermos

This is an important detail: Pemberton fills his tea every evening at 21:00. The drug was placed in the cup AFTER Pemberton had poured his tea. So the killer entered the office and placed the drug after Pemberton poured his tea but before he drank it (or before the first sips).

Gloves were used. Premeditated murder.

choices.text: Who uses gloves in the hospital?

scenes.addCharacterNote: Drug placed in the cup, not the thermos. Gloves were used. Premeditated murder. The killer must have entered the office between 21:00-21:30.

scenes.text:
You read Pemberton's locked diary. The last entries:

'March 3: I noticed inconsistencies in Evelyn's prescription records. She prescribed higher doses than necessary to some patients. Why? I will investigate.'

'March 5: Marcus's research has completely spiraled out of control. If the ethics board found out, they would revoke his research license. I warned him, he didn't listen.'

'March 7: Agnes said strange things today. She spoke of the old days. She may know about the 1994 incident. I must be careful.'

'March 9: Someone entered my office, went through my files. Who? Thomas has a key but why would he? Catherine could also have accessed the office during her night shift.'

Pemberton was suspicious of everyone. And someone broke into his office 2 days before his death.
scenes.addCharacterNote: Pemberton was suspicious about 5 different matters: Evelyn's excessive dosages, Marcus's unethical research, Agnes's 1994 secret, unauthorized office entry. Everyone has something to hide.

scenes.text:
You search the office more thoroughly.

Under the desk: A small piece of paper. Handwritten on it: 'E.M. - prescription #4471, #4489, #4502 - check'

Pemberton's computer: The last opened file is 'Prescription_Audit_2026.xlsx'. It lists Evelyn Marsh's prescription records for the last 6 months. Some rows are highlighted in red.

File cabinet: The 'Victor Ashbourne - Treatment Plan' file is at the front. Inside are dosage changes signed by Evelyn. Thorazine dosages increasing every month.

In the waste bin: An empty tea packet (Earl Grey) and a scrap of paper reading: 'C.V. - night report - check'. Catherine Vale's initials?

On the window sill: A small piece of soil. It could have fallen from someone's shoe who came from the garden.
scenes.addCharacterNote: Pemberton was investigating Evelyn's prescriptions. Victor's dosage keeps increasing. Catherine's night reports to be checked. Soil on the window sill.

scenes.text:
You go down to the hospital pharmacy. In the basement, a cold and sterile room. Pharmacist Mr. Phillips greets you.

'I checked the Thorazine stock, Detective. 800 milligrams missing. Exactly the lethal dose.'

Pharmacy access card records:
- Dr. Evelyn Marsh: 3 times in the last week (routine prescription processing)
- Dr. Marcus Whitfield: 2 times in the last week (research samples)
- Catherine Vale: Every day (medication distribution)
- Thomas Hargrove: 2 days ago (air conditioning repair)

Mr. Phillips adds: 'The system is old. Magnetic card-based. Can it be cloned? Theoretically, yes. But you can also get in without a card -- there's a physical key too. Thomas has a key for maintenance.'

choices.text: Get details on Evelyn's pharmacy visits

scenes.addCharacterNote: 800mg Thorazine missing from the pharmacy. 4 people can access the pharmacy: Evelyn, Marcus, Catherine, Thomas. System is old, card can be cloned.

scenes.text:
You investigate the glove issue. People who use gloves in the hospital:

- All doctors and nurses: Latex/nitrile medical gloves (available everywhere)
- Thomas Hargrove: Garden and work gloves (personal)
- Cleaning crew: Rubber gloves

Forensics team note: 'The cup was not touched, no glove marks. The drug was probably poured directly into the cup without touching the rim. This is a precise operation, the work of someone who knows the drug.'

When Thomas's garden shed was searched: A very small amount of Thorazine residue was detected on the inner surface of his left work glove.

Thomas's explanation: 'I touched the drug boxes while repairing the pharmacy air conditioning.'

choices.text: Check the pharmacy AC repair log

scenes.addCharacterNote: Thorazine residue on Thomas's glove. Contaminated on the INNER surface, cannot be explained by external contact. Anyone can take gloves from the shed.

scenes.text:
You examine Evelyn's prescription records. Data pulled from the pharmacy system:

Thorazine prescriptions written by Dr. Evelyn Marsh in the last 6 months:
- Victor Ashbourne: Gradual increase. Starting at 200mg, now 450mg. 15-20% increase each month.
- Patient #12 Margaret Collins: 30% above average
- Patient #28 David Palmer: 25% above average
- Patient #33 Lisa Chen: 35% above average

Total excess Thorazine prescribed: approximately 2400mg (over 6 months). Where did this excess medication go?

The red-highlighted rows on Pemberton's computer show exactly these prescriptions. Pemberton had discovered this.

choices.text: Talk to Evelyn about the prescriptions

scenes.addCharacterNote: Evelyn has been prescribing excess Thorazine for 6 months. Total 2400mg excess. Lethal dose is 800mg. Pemberton had discovered this.

scenes.text:
You enter Marcus's research laboratory. A sterile, neatly organized room. Experiment data and computer screens on the desks.

You find locked files in a hidden file cabinet:

- 12 patients were given experimental drugs without notifying the ethics board
- 3 patients experienced serious side effects: hallucinations, memory loss, increased aggression
- Results were falsified and published in an academic journal
- A note: 'Pemberton knows. He said either you end it or I will. Time is running out.'

Marcus's laboratory access card: Entry at 19:00 on the night of the incident, exit at 23:15. But there are no cameras inside the lab.

There is a back staircase from the lab to the pharmacy. Direct access to the basement floor.
scenes.addCharacterNote: Marcus conducted unethical experiments. 12 patients, 3 with side effects. Pemberton knew and was threatening him. Back staircase from lab to pharmacy.

scenes.text:
You investigate the 1994 incident. In the hospital archives:

1994, Blackmoor Asylum: A 14-year-old patient, Timothy Walsh, died during treatment. Official record: 'treatment complication'. Case closed.

But from old newspapers: 'Child Death Controversy at Blackmoor' - the family filed a lawsuit but the evidence was found insufficient. The family received compensation, case was closed.

Timothy's treating doctor: Young Dr. Richard Pemberton.

Agnes was also at the hospital at that time. Former chief doctor Dr. Harold Finch (died in 1998) wrote a letter to Agnes: 'Stay silent, it's better for everyone.'

Agnes has known the true cause of a child's death for 30 years.

choices.text: Examine Timothy Walsh's file in detail

scenes.addCharacterNote: 14-year-old patient Timothy Walsh died in 1994. His doctor was young Pemberton. Agnes knows about this. A 30-year-old secret.

scenes.text:
You investigate the unauthorized office entry.

Pemberton's office door: Electronic lock + physical key. Electronic lock card records:

Last 1 week:
- Pemberton: Every day (normal)
- Evelyn Marsh: 2 times (meetings)
- Catherine Vale: 1 time (night report delivery)
- Cleaning: Every morning (normal)

But: Physical key entries are not recorded. Thomas has spare keys to all doors. Agnes is also rumored to have a secret set of keys.

Pemberton wrote in his 'March 9' note that 'my files have been rummaged through'. Who had the motivation to go through the files? Everyone.

Interesting detail: Catherine's night report delivery was at 02:00. Is that normal?

choices.text: Question Thomas about his key access
Question Catherine about her night visit

scenes.addCharacterNote: Evelyn, Catherine, and cleaning entered Pemberton's office in the last week (by card). Physical key entries are not logged. Thomas and Agnes have keys.

scenes.text:
You examine Victor Ashbourne's treatment plan file.

Diagnosis: Dissociative identity disorder + severe depression
Treatment duration: 2 years
Attending physician: Dr. Evelyn Marsh

Thorazine dosage history:
- Start: 200mg/day
- After 3 months: 250mg
- After 6 months: 300mg
- After 9 months: 350mg
- After 12 months: 400mg
- Now: 450mg/day (borderline value)

Evelyn's notes: 'Patient is resistant. Dosage increase is a clinical necessity.'

But: No independent psychiatric opinion was requested. Victor himself doesn't seem to be aware of the dosage increases.

Victor is a former university professor. Intelligent, could be aware, could be deceived. Why didn't he question his treatment?

choices.text: Talk to Evelyn about the dosage

scenes.addCharacterNote: Victor's Thorazine dose increased from 200mg to 450mg over 2 years. Evelyn's decision, no independent opinion sought. Victor seems unaware.

scenes.text:
You have the soil on the window analyzed.

Lab result: The soil sample is from the greenhouse area of the hospital garden. Contains a mix of fertilizer and greenhouse soil. Fresh, deposited within the last 24-48 hours.

Thomas Hargrove is responsible for greenhouse maintenance. But what reason would Thomas have to enter the office?

Additional info: The greenhouse is behind the hospital building. From the greenhouse to inside the building: through the back door to the basement, then up the stairs. This route is NOT covered by security cameras.

Thomas knows this route. So does Agnes (she's been here for 30 years). And Victor's cell window overlooks the garden.
scenes.addCharacterNote: Greenhouse soil on Pemberton's office window. Camera-free passage from greenhouse to basement exists. Thomas, Agnes know this route.

scenes.text:
You examine Catherine Vale's medication distribution logbook in detail.

Normal course: Patient check and medication distribution every 30 minutes, every step recorded.

20:30 - Patient #12 Margaret Collins - Thorazine 150mg - OK
20:45 - Patient #28 David Palmer - Risperidone 4mg - OK
21:00 - Patient #33 Lisa Chen - Clozapine 200mg - OK
21:10 - [GAP BEGINS]
21:30 - Patient #47 Theodore Morrison - Anticonvulsant (emergency) - NOTE: epileptic seizure
21:45 - Patient #51 Robert Hayes - Lorazepam 2mg - OK

20-minute gap. Catherine's explanation: Patient #47 had a crisis.

Patient #47 Theodore Morrison: An epilepsy patient who has seizures. But the seizure is only reported by Catherine, no independent verification.

choices.text: Talk to Catherine about the 20 minutes

scenes.addCharacterNote: 20-min gap in Catherine's log between 21:10-21:30. Patient #47 had a seizure but Catherine is the only source. Gap coincides exactly with time of death.

scenes.text:
You go to Thomas's garden shed. A small, narrow room. Tools, fertilizers, seeds.

The left work glove is being examined. Forensics report:

'Low amounts of Thorazine residue on the inner surface of the glove. Contamination of the INNER surface from EXTERNAL contact is not expected. The drug was contaminated from the INSIDE, meaning either the person wearing the glove had it on their hand or from the inside of an object held with the glove.'

Thomas's explanation: 'Boxes fell over in the pharmacy, I cleaned my hands and put on the glove.'

Pharmacist Mr. Phillips: 'Yes, boxes fell over. But Thomas held them from the OUTSIDE, the inner surface was not in contact.'

This glove resembles that of someone who held Thorazine tablets. But the shed is not locked, anyone can enter.

choices.text: Check who else entered the shed
Investigate the relationship between Agnes and Thomas

scenes.addCharacterNote: Thomas's glove contaminated with Thorazine on inner surface. Pharmacist debunks his explanation. Shed is unlocked, someone else could have used it.

scenes.text:
You examine the pharmacy AC repair log.

Maintenance log: Thomas Hargrove repaired the pharmacy air conditioning 2 days ago (March 7). Work duration: 14:00-16:30.

Pharmacist Mr. Phillips: 'Yes, the AC was broken. Thomas came, fixed it. Some drug boxes fell off the shelves, Thomas helped.'

But: Thomas was ALONE in the pharmacy for 2.5 hours. Mr. Phillips: 'I went out for lunch, no problem with Thomas working alone, I trust him.'

Thomas alone in the pharmacy for 2.5 hours. He could have taken Thorazine. But 800mg is a large amount: approximately 16 tablets. Would it be noticed?

Mr. Phillips: 'We do stock counts once a month. If 16 tablets went missing in between, we wouldn't notice right away.'

choices.text: Examine pharmacy stock records in detail

scenes.addCharacterNote: Thomas was alone in the pharmacy for 2.5 hours. Could have taken Thorazine. Stock counts are monthly, 16 missing tablets wouldn't be noticed immediately.

scenes.text:
You find Dr. Evelyn Marsh in her office. A sterile, minimalist room. Diplomas on the wall, neatly arranged files on the desk. Evelyn greets you coldly but professionally.

'Detective, I'm here to help you. Richard's death shook all of us.'

She takes a sip of her coffee. Her hand doesn't tremble.

'I was in my office at the time of the incident. Writing patient reports. My computer logs confirm this. I went to the restroom around 21:15, returned 4 minutes later. That's it.'

Evelyn is prepared for every question. Every answer is measured. She has 30 years of psychiatry experience, reading people is her job. But you're a detective too.

choices.text: The restroom was closed at that time, you knew that
About the inconsistencies in your prescription records
How was your relationship with Pemberton?
Can you describe the night of the incident in detail?

scenes.addCharacterNote: Evelyn is cold and composed. Said she was in her office, went to the restroom at 21:15. Prepared for everything.

scenes.text:
You show Evelyn the prescription records.

'In the last 6 months, you've prescribed above-normal Thorazine to patients. A total of 2400mg excess. Where did this medication go?'

Evelyn's eyes narrow for a moment, then she puts her professional mask back on.

'Every patient is different, Detective. Increasing dosage for patients with growing resistance is standard practice. Any psychiatrist knows this.'

'But Pemberton was investigating this. He had highlighted your prescriptions in red on his computer.'

Evelyn pauses for a moment. 'Richard... was a meticulous man. He checked everything. But my prescriptions are clinical necessities. Any independent expert would confirm this.'

Evelyn's defense is consistent, but an independent expert opinion has not yet been evaluated.

choices.text: The pharmacology report says your dosages are dangerous
Can you explain Victor Ashbourne's dosages?
Was Pemberton trying to expose you?

scenes.addCharacterNote: Evelyn defends prescription inconsistencies as 'clinical necessity'. She was aware of Pemberton's investigation.

scenes.text:
The independent pharmacology expert Prof. Sarah Chen's report has arrived.

Findings:

'The Thorazine dosages prescribed by Dr. Evelyn Marsh in the last 6 months were examined. The dosage prescribed to Victor Ashbourne shows a gradual increase. The current 450mg/day dose is at the borderline value for the patient's weight.'

'Three other patients were also prescribed 25-35% above average. This can be clinically explained, BUT requires an independent opinion. Why was a second opinion not requested?'

'Important: Thorazine was withdrawn from the pharmacy on the night of the incident using Evelyn's prescription system password. Time: 20:45. This is outside Evelyn's routine hours. However, the password may have been shared.'

Prof. Chen: 'Either there are resistant patients, or there's another purpose. We need to look at the evidence.'

choices.text: Confront Evelyn: drugs were withdrawn using your password last night
Could the password have been used by someone else?
Marcus also has prescription authority, what about his password?

scenes.addCharacterNote: Pharmacology report: Evelyn's dosages are borderline. Thorazine withdrawn from pharmacy using Evelyn's password on the night of the incident (20:45). Password may have been shared.

scenes.text:
You examine the back staircase leading from the lab to the pharmacy.

The staircase starts at the laboratory back door and ends on the basement floor, 10 meters from the pharmacy. There are NO cameras on this staircase.

Marcus knows this staircase (he works in the lab). Thomas knows too (he does basement maintenance). Does Catherine know? Probably -- you learn every corner during night shifts.

You search for traces on the stairs: Clean. But at the basement exit of the staircase, there's an old door with a rusty lock. It looks like it was recently opened, rust fragments on the floor.

Anyone who passed through this door could reach the pharmacy without being on camera.

choices.text: Question Marcus: did you use this staircase?
Does Thomas know about this staircase?

scenes.addCharacterNote: Camera-free back staircase from lab to pharmacy. Door rust is broken, recently used. Marcus, Thomas, Catherine know this route.

scenes.text:
You investigate the patients in Marcus's experimental treatment program.

Patient #19 Robert Mills (48, schizophrenia): 'Dr. Whitfield gave us a new drug. He said it was different. Some nights I felt terrible, but when I told the doctor, he adjusted the dose.'

Patient #24 Anne Foster (35, bipolar): 'My hallucinations increased. Dr. Whitfield dismissed it as normal. Then it started to decrease. But it was very frightening during that period.'

Patient #31 James Cooper (55, depression): 'I didn't sign anything. They didn't show me a consent form. One day they gave me a different pill, that's it.'

None of these patients signed consent forms. The ethics violation is serious.

choices.text: Talk to the ethics board chairman

scenes.addCharacterNote: None of Marcus's 12 experimental patients signed consent forms. 3 experienced serious side effects. Ethics violation confirmed.

scenes.text:
You find Dr. Marcus Whitfield in his laboratory. Thick-rimmed glasses, graying hair, tired eyes. Experiment equipment all around him.

'Detective, welcome. Pemberton's death is tragic. But you should know that we didn't get along. I won't hide it.'

Marcus speaks directly, not trying to conceal anything:

'Pemberton cut my research funding. He was going to trash 20 years of my work. Do I have a motive? Yes. But am I a killer? No.'

He takes a sip of coffee from the mug on his desk.

'I worked here the night of the incident. Arrived at 19:00, left at 23:15. Lab card records confirm it.'

choices.text: But you were seen in the corridor, your claim of being in the lab is a lie
What exactly did you argue about with Pemberton?
Can you describe the night of the incident in detail?

scenes.addCharacterNote: Marcus is candid: he admits having a motive but claims he's not the killer. Said he was working in the lab.

scenes.text:
You find Nurse Janet Powell. An experienced night nurse with 15 years of service.

'Yes, Detective. I saw Dr. Whitfield in the West Wing corridor around 21:20. He was walking with a coffee mug. We exchanged greetings.'

'Which direction was he heading?'

'He was going toward the cafeteria. Normal thing, doctors get coffee at night. But...' she pauses.

'But?'

'He was in a hurry. Normally Marcus walks slowly, he's a thoughtful man. But that night he was walking fast. And... he slowed down for a moment when passing in front of Pemberton's office. Then kept going.'

Janet is a reliable witness. 15 years of experience, an eye for detail.

choices.text: Did you see anyone else at that time?
Did you see Catherine that night?

scenes.addCharacterNote: Nurse Janet Powell: Saw Marcus in the West Wing corridor at 21:20. He was in a hurry. Slowed down in front of Pemberton's office.

scenes.text:
You find Sister Agnes Blackwood in the chapel. Sitting among old wooden pews by candlelight. Rosary beads in her hand.

'Detective... God sent you. I was praying for Richard's soul.'

She stands up. 63 years old but upright, hard-faced.

'I've been in this hospital for 30 years. I visit patients every day. I give them spiritual support. Richard... was a complicated man. Well-intentioned but sometimes made wrong decisions.'

She narrows her eyes: 'On the night of the incident, I was here praying. Same time every evening. God is my witness.'

Agnes appears calm. But can someone carrying a 30-year-old secret be this calm?

choices.text: The chapel was locked at 20:00, you were seen in the cafeteria at 20:30
What happened to Timothy Walsh in 1994?

scenes.text:
You ask Agnes about the 1994 incident.

'Timothy Walsh...' Agnes's eyes grow moist. 'That child... he was 14 years old. He'd been diagnosed with schizophrenia but I was always doubtful. Too young.'

She grips her rosary tightly.

'Pemberton was a young doctor then. He applied an aggressive treatment to Timothy. The boy reacted, had a seizure and... died.' Her voice trembles.

'The official report said treatment complication. But I was there, Detective. Pemberton miscalculated the dosage. Negligence. But Dr. Finch covered everything up.'

'I stayed silent for 30 years. I kept the letter. But recently... Richard started talking about the old days. As if he was suffering from guilt.'

scenes.text:
You examine Timothy Walsh's archive file.

Patient: Timothy Walsh, age 14
Diagnosis: Early-onset schizophrenia
Treating doctor: Dr. Richard Pemberton
Date of death: April 15, 1994
Cause of death: Treatment complication (official)

Details: Timothy was administered a high dose of haloperidol. He had a seizure, his heart stopped. Resuscitation failed.

Family lawsuit: The Walsh family sued the hospital. Settled with a 50,000 pound compensation. Family's lawyer: 'The dosage was too high for a child. There was negligence.'

Dr. Finch's incident report: 'Unexpected complication. Dr. Pemberton acted according to protocol.' This report is controversial.

Agnes is not on the witness list but was at the hospital during the incident.

'Drugs on my gloves?' Thomas looks at his hands. 'While I was repairing in the pharmacy... boxes fell over. I washed my hands and put on my glove.'

Thomas's face reddens. 'I... I don't know. Maybe I didn't wash my hands well enough. Maybe someone else used my gloves. The shed isn't locked.'

You find Catherine Vale at the nurse station. Young, tired, dark circles under her eyes. A file in her hand.

'Detective, today is a very busy day. The patients are restless, they heard about Pemberton's death.'

'On the night of the incident, I was distributing medication. My usual routine. But Patient #47 Theodore Morrison had an epileptic seizure. I had to intervene for 20 minutes. I couldn't keep records during that time.'

Catherine is professional but tired. Night shifts have worn her down.

'Pemberton... was a good man. He asked me to compile some data. Patient statistics, treatment outcomes. A secret project.'

'How long did Catherine stay with you?'

'I don't know. There's no sense of time during a seizure. But when I woke up, I looked at the clock, it was 21:35. Catherine was still with me.'

scenes.text:
You enter Victor Ashbourne's cell. A small, white-walled room. A bed, a desk, books on a shelf. Victor sits by the window, looking out at the garden.

'Detective! Someone finally came. I'm bored in this cell.' He laughs.

Victor is intelligent, sharp-eyed. A former university professor. Even with his messy hair and two-day stubble, he has an intellectual air.

'Pemberton is dead, huh? I'm sorry. He was a good doctor, actually. He listened to me. Unlike Evelyn.'

He raises his eyebrows: 'Evelyn prescribes me drugs, checks on me and leaves. Pemberton would TALK to me. Who will talk now?'

Victor shows his monitor: 'You see, I have this on my ankle. I can't leave my cell. My door is locked. How can I be the killer?'

Marcus, after a long silence.

'I wanted to talk to him. About my research. I was going to beg him: give me one more chance. Go to the ethics board yourself, let me fix it.'

He takes off his glasses. 'But I didn't have the courage. The light was on, he was inside. I was going to knock but... I stopped. I moved on. Got my coffee, went back to the lab.'

'Why didn't you have the courage?'

'Because Richard was right. I made a mistake. And accepting that... means accepting the end of a 30-year career.'

Marcus looks broken. Strong motive but he was going to beg Pemberton, not kill him.
scenes.addCharacterNote: Marcus was going to beg Pemberton but didn't have the courage. Admits his mistake. Looks broken.

scenes.text:
'Catherine, you changed your route that night. You normally start from B Wing but you went through the West Wing first.'

Catherine hesitates. 'I... was leaving my night report at Pemberton's office. I do it every night. I leave it on his desk.'

'Did you enter Pemberton's office that night?'

'No! The door was closed, I slid the report under the door. I always do that. It's normal.'

'But you don't appear in the door records that night.'

'Because I didn't OPEN the door. I slid the report underneath. That doesn't create a record.'

Catherine's explanation is logical: sliding paper under a door doesn't create a record. But being in the West Wing places her near the crime scene at the time of death.

choices.text: Let's talk about the secret project

scenes.addCharacterNote: Catherine said she slid the report under Pemberton's door. Didn't enter. Logical but she was at the crime scene.

scenes.text:
'Sister Agnes, can anyone confirm you were in the chapel between 20:45 and 22:05?'

Agnes shakes her head. 'I pray alone in the chapel. God is my witness, but He doesn't testify in court.'

A faint smile.

'Detective, I am a 63-year-old nun. I have neither the knowledge nor the intention to poison Pemberton. Where would I know the Thorazine dosage?'

'You've been working in the hospital for 30 years. You know the medications.'

Agnes pauses. 'I know the names. Dosages? No. I'm not a doctor, I'm a chaplain. I provide spiritual support, not medication.'

Agnes's medical knowledge is limited. But if she had collaborated with Thomas, Thomas could provide pharmacy access and Agnes could provide office access.

choices.text: Let's investigate your relationship with Thomas

scenes.addCharacterNote: Agnes was alone in the chapel, no one to confirm. Says her medical knowledge is limited. Thomas collaboration theoretically possible.

scenes.text:
'Did Pemberton bring up the 1994 subject recently?'

Agnes nods. 'Yes. 2 weeks ago. He called me to his office. He talked about Timothy. His eyes were moist.'

'What did he say?'

'He said I made a mistake. I ruined that child's life. I can't ask for forgiveness, but at least I can do the right thing.'

'What was the right thing?'

'I don't know. But I think... he wanted to fix some things at the hospital. Maybe stop Marcus's experiments. Maybe investigate Evelyn's prescriptions. Maybe discharge Victor.'

Pemberton was suffering from guilt and wanted to fix the hospital. This threatened everyone: Evelyn, Marcus, maybe even Agnes.

choices.text: How did this information affect you?

scenes.addCharacterNote: Pemberton was suffering from guilt. Confessed about 1994 to Agnes. Wanted to fix the hospital. This was a threat to everyone.

scenes.text:
'What do you know about Emily Hargrove?'

Agnes's eyes darken. 'Emily... a beautiful girl. She had bipolar disorder but was improving. I visited her every day.'

'Pemberton discharged her early.'

'Yes. Budget cuts. I objected but he didn't listen. I said Emily wasn't ready. 3 months later... she committed suicide.'

Agnes wipes her eyes. 'Thomas never forgot this. He came here for Emily. I know that. I helped him too. I wrote the reference letter.'

'Could Thomas have killed Pemberton?'

Agnes, firmly: 'Thomas couldn't kill a fly. He just wanted to be close to his sister's memory.'
scenes.addCharacterNote: Agnes knew Emily and objected to early discharge. Defends Thomas: 'couldn't kill a fly' she says.

scenes.text:
'How did you open Pemberton's office door?'

Agnes pauses. 'The door... was open. It wasn't locked. I pushed and it opened.'

'Electronic lock records show the door wasn't opened until 22:05. Did you open it?'

'Yes, I opened it with my card. At 22:05.'

'But you just said the door was open.'

Agnes hesitates. 'I... got confused. I opened it with my card. I was in a panic, it's hard to remember.'

Agnes opened the door with her card (22:05 record). But when she entered, Pemberton was already dead (death between 21:30-22:00). Agnes is the person who found the body, not the killer. This timing clears Agnes.
scenes.addCharacterNote: Agnes opened the door with her card at 22:05. Pemberton had died between 21:30-22:00. Agnes is the person who found the body.

scenes.text:
'Did Pemberton's guilt relieve you or anger you?'

Agnes thinks for a long time.

'Both. I was relieved because he finally accepted it. But I was angry because 30 years had passed. Timothy's family suffered for 30 years. What good is a late confession?'

'Did you want revenge?'

Agnes lifts her head. 'Did I want revenge? Every night for 30 years, I did. But I made a promise to God: revenge is not my business. I will forgive. And I forgave.'

Her eyes fill with tears. 'I didn't kill Pemberton. If I had, it would have trashed my 30 years of forgiveness. It would have destroyed my soul.'

Agnes's argument is strong: a 30-year spiritual struggle. But emotional decision-makers sometimes act beyond logic.
scenes.addCharacterNote: Agnes wanted revenge but promised God. Wouldn't trash her 30 years of forgiveness. Strong argument.

scenes.text:
'Did you pass in front of Pemberton's office while wandering the corridors?'

Agnes pauses. 'Yes... I passed by. Light was coming from under the door. I didn't knock. I was on my way to pray.'

'What time was it?'

'21:00... maybe 21:15. I'm not sure.'

'At 21:15, Evelyn was also seen in the corridor.'

Agnes raises her eyebrows. 'Evelyn? I didn't see her. Maybe she was in her office when I passed. Or we passed at different times.'

Agnes being in the corridor overlaps with Evelyn's timeline. Both were in the West Wing corridor around 21:15. They didn't see each other.
scenes.addCharacterNote: Agnes passed in front of Pemberton's office around 21:15. Evelyn also in the corridor at the same time. Both in the same corridor but didn't see each other.

scenes.text:
'Agnes, you called the Walsh family 2 months ago. Why?'

Agnes looks surprised. 'You... reached them?'

'Margaret Walsh told us. You said Pemberton was suffering from guilt.'

Agnes bows her head. 'I owe that family for 30 years. I couldn't do anything for Timothy. At least I could tell his mother about Pemberton's remorse.'

'Did you also consider exposing Pemberton?'

A long silence. 'Yes. I thought about it. But Pemberton was already starting to fix things himself. Exposure would have been unnecessary.'

Was Agnes's call to the Walsh family out of guilt or part of a revenge plan? The timing is suspicious: 2 months before Pemberton's death.
scenes.addCharacterNote: Agnes says she called the Walsh family out of guilt. Considered exposing Pemberton but found it 'unnecessary'. Timing is suspicious.

scenes.text:
You ask Cleaner Maria about Evelyn's direction.

'Dr. Marsh was coming toward the restroom. But when she saw it was closed... she stopped. Then turned right.'

'Right? What's on the right side?'

'On the right... the archive room and Pemberton's office.'

Maria's observation is critical: Evelyn turned right when the restroom was closed. On the right side are both the archive room and Pemberton's office. Evelyn claims she went to the archive room, but the carpet fibers tell a different story.

Maria: 'I cleaned the restroom, then left. I didn't see Evelyn again.'
scenes.addCharacterNote: Maria: Evelyn turned right when restroom was closed. Right side has the archive room AND Pemberton's office.

scenes.text:
You request the carpet fiber analysis report from the forensics team.

Report:

'The dark burgundy carpet fibers from the sole of Dr. Evelyn Marsh's office shoes were analyzed. The fibers are a wool-polyester blend, hand-woven. Color code: Burgundy-2204.'

'Rooms in the hospital with the same carpet:
1. Pemberton's office (antique carpet, MATCH)
2. Meeting room (different color code: Burgundy-2201, NO MATCH)
3. Library (different brand, NO MATCH)
4. Deputy director's office (same brand but different texture, NO MATCH)'

Result: The fibers ONLY match the carpet in Pemberton's office. The other 3 rooms are eliminated.

This evidence has become stronger. But Evelyn has routine meetings with Pemberton. When did the fibers transfer? The night of the incident, or earlier?
scenes.addCharacterNote: Carpet fiber ONLY matches Pemberton's office. Other rooms eliminated. Evelyn entered Pemberton's room recently but when is unclear. She has routine meetings.

scenes.text:
'What documents were you collecting, Thomas?'

'Pemberton's early discharge decisions. There are other patients like Emily. In 3 years, 12 patients were discharged early. 4 of them had subsequent problems.'

'Where did you find these documents?'

'From the archive room. I have a key, as maintenance worker I can enter everywhere. I copied them at night when nobody was around.'

'Did you enter Pemberton's office on March 9?'

Thomas, after a long silence. 'Yes. I went through his files. I was looking for Emily's discharge form. I found it and copied it.'

Thomas admitted he entered the office. This explains the 'someone entered my office' note in Pemberton's diary. But Thomas's purpose was document collection, not murder.

choices.text: Will you show me the documents?

scenes.addCharacterNote: Thomas entered Pemberton's office and copied files. Was collecting early discharge documents. Purpose is exposure, not murder.

scenes.text:
'Did Agnes help you collect these documents?'

Thomas shakes his head. 'No. Agnes doesn't even know. This is my personal business.'

'Agnes has keys, she could have helped you.'

'Agnes is a good woman but I didn't involve her in this. My fight, my responsibility.'

Thomas is clear: he didn't involve Agnes. This weakens the Agnes-Thomas collaboration theory.

But Thomas's solo document-collecting operation is also suspicious: entering offices at night, copying files. Not for murder, but illegal.
scenes.addCharacterNote: Thomas said he didn't involve Agnes. Was collecting documents alone. Agnes-Thomas collaboration weakening.

scenes.text:
'What would Evelyn have done if she found out?'

Catherine trembles. 'Evelyn... is a difficult woman. Control freak. If she found out, she would have had me fired from the hospital.'

'Anything else?'

'Evelyn likes to manage people. She's very authoritarian about Victor's treatment, doesn't even feel the need for an independent opinion.'

'Could Evelyn have killed Pemberton?'

Catherine, after a long silence. 'I don't know. Everyone had a reason. Marcus's career was at stake, Agnes has 30 years of anger, Thomas wanted revenge for his sister. Evelyn also has a motive but she's not the only suspect.'

Catherine speaks carefully. Is she not directly accusing anyone, or is she deflecting attention from herself?
scenes.addCharacterNote: Catherine speaks carefully about Evelyn. Said everyone has a motive. Didn't directly accuse anyone.

scenes.text:
You search for the data Catherine compiled for Pemberton.

In Catherine's cabinet, a file labeled: 'R.P. - Secret'.

Inside:
- Evelyn's Thorazine prescriptions for the last 6 months (highlighted in red)
- Pharmacy withdrawal records (Evelyn's entries highlighted)
- Victor Ashbourne dosage graph (showing upward trend)
- Catherine's note: 'Evelyn is systematically prescribing excess Thorazine. Total excess: ~2400mg. This is a deliberate decision. Reported to Pemberton.'

This file confirms Catherine was working with Pemberton. But it also shows that Catherine knew Evelyn's prescription pattern very well. What could Catherine have done with this knowledge?
scenes.addCharacterNote: Catherine documented Evelyn's prescriptions for Pemberton. 2400mg excess Thorazine recorded. Catherine knows Evelyn's pattern intimately.

scenes.text:
'Victor, does Evelyn visit you at night?'

Victor thinks. 'Evelyn? No. Evelyn only comes during office hours. She gives my medication, leaves. Catherine gives the nighttime medication.'

'She never came at night?'

'Once... maybe 1 month ago. She came at night, woke me from sleep. Measured my blood pressure, gave medication. It wasn't an unusual visit. I asked why she came, she said emergency.'

'What was the emergency?'

'I don't know. But that night I felt very bad. Maybe she changed the dosage.'

Evelyn visited Victor at night. Dosage adjustment or another purpose?
scenes.addCharacterNote: Evelyn visited Victor at night 1 month ago. Said 'emergency' but Victor felt bad. Dosage change?

scenes.text:
'Victor, why would Evelyn want to keep you in the hospital?'

Victor looks out the window at the garden.

'I've been thinking... I'm a project for Evelyn. Dissociative identity disorder is a rare diagnosis. Evelyn is doing research on me. If I'm discharged, the research ends.'

'So she's using you as a lab rat?'

'A lab rat? Maybe. Or maybe she really is trying to treat me. I can't tell. But Pemberton was going to get me out, and Evelyn opposed it.'

Victor's observation is interesting: Evelyn may see Victor as research material. If Victor is discharged, both Evelyn's research and her reason for prescribing excess Thorazine end.
scenes.addCharacterNote: Victor thinks Evelyn sees him as research material. If discharged, Evelyn's project ends.

scenes.text:
'Pemberton was going to fix some things. What do you think that means, Victor?'

Victor is thoughtful.

'Pemberton had changed recently. More humane, more careful. As if he was trying to pay for his past.'

'I think he was going to investigate my treatment by Evelyn. Maybe Marcus's experiments too. Maybe he was going to do something about the old events Agnes knows about.'

'And you would be discharged.'

Victor smiles. 'Yes. I had hope for the first time. But now Pemberton is dead and I'm condemned to stay here. At Evelyn's mercy.'

Victor has NO motive: Pemberton's death harmed him, didn't benefit him.
scenes.addCharacterNote: Pemberton's death was a disaster for Victor. Lost his hope of discharge. Left at Evelyn's mercy. He has NO motive.

scenes.text:
'Did Marcus visit you?'

'Yes, 2 months ago. It was a strange visit. Normally Marcus doesn't come to see me. He came, asked questions: when do my hallucinations start, how do I feel after medications, what are the side effects.'

'For what purpose?'

'I don't know. But he wasn't asking like a doctor, he was asking like a researcher. He was taking notes. Maybe he wanted to include me in his experimental program.'

'What did you tell Marcus?'

'I told him everything. Why wouldn't I? But then I told Pemberton. Pemberton was very angry. He said Marcus has no right to ask you questions.'

Marcus had targeted Victor for research but Pemberton stopped him. This escalated the Marcus-Pemberton tension.
scenes.addCharacterNote: Marcus targeted Victor for research. Pemberton stopped him and was very angry. Marcus-Pemberton tension escalated.

scenes.text:
'Tell me about your relationship with Agnes.'

Victor smiles. 'Agnes is my only friend here. She comes every day. Prays, reads books. Sometimes we just sit.'

'What do you think about her?'

'Agnes is a good person. But she's suffering. Something is inside her, she's been carrying it for years. I once caught her crying. She said a name: Timothy. I asked who Timothy was, she didn't answer.'

Victor has sensed Agnes's secret but doesn't know it. Agnes is close to Victor but couldn't share the Timothy secret.

'Could Agnes have killed Pemberton?'

Victor thinks for a long time. 'Agnes... I don't know. Someone who suffers that much could snap one day. But I think she truly forgave Pemberton.'
scenes.addCharacterNote: Victor loves Agnes but senses her pain. Heard the name Timothy but doesn't know. Agnes could snap but he thinks she truly forgave.

scenes.text:
You look for the report Catherine slid under the door.

In front of Pemberton's office door: Yes, there is a paper. Slid under the door. Catherine's night report, date is correct.

Report contents: Patient checks, medication distribution record, night events. A normal night report.

This supports Catherine's explanation: she slid the report under the door, didn't enter.

But timing is important: When did Catherine come here? The report has a time written on it: 21:20.

At 21:20, Catherine was in the West Wing and dropped off the report. Then she headed toward B Wing. This matches Janet's observation: Catherine was seen heading toward B Wing at 21:25.
scenes.addCharacterNote: Catherine's report found, timestamped 21:20. Supports her explanation. Catherine's innocence strengthens.

scenes.text:
Thomas shows the documents he collected.

A file: 'Blackmoor Early Discharge Cases'

- Emily Hargrove: Bipolar, early discharge, suicide (3 months later)
- Robert Mills: Schizophrenia, early discharge, homelessness (6 months later)
- David Palmer: Severe depression, early discharge, rehospitalization (2 months later)
- 9 other cases: Early discharge due to budget cuts

Thomas: 'I was going to give these documents to a journalist. Pemberton should have been held accountable. But through legal channels.'

Thomas's purpose is clear: exposure, not revenge. This doesn't completely remove him from the suspect list but weakens his motive.
scenes.addCharacterNote: Thomas documented 12 early discharge cases. Was going to give to journalist. Purpose is legal exposure. Murder motive weakening.

scenes.text:
You've gathered enough evidence. Now it's decision time, Detective.

The truth awaits you in the dark corridors of Blackmoor. Who do you accuse?

endings.title: Kusursuz Adalet
endings.description: Dr. Evelyn Marsh was arrested as the killer of Dr. Richard Pemberton. All evidence is complete.
endings.epilogueText: Dr. Evelyn Marsh was arrested. Here is the true story: Evelyn had been prescribing excessive Thorazine doses to Blackmoor patients for years, selling the surplus on the black market for additional income. Pharmacy records and prescription logs proved this scheme. Dr. Pemberton noticed the numbers didn't add up during a routine drug audit and began questioning Evelyn. Pemberton's last diary note explained everything: 'There are serious discrepancies between Evelyn's prescriptions and pharmacy records. I will report to management tomorrow.' Evelyn acted that night. Taking advantage of the gap in the cleaning schedule, she entered Pemberton's room and administered the lethal dose from her stockpiled Thorazine. The carpet fibers on her shoes proved she was at the scene, and the pharmacology report proved she had been stockpiling drugs for months. A planned and cold-blooded murder. The court sentenced Evelyn to life imprisonment. Blackmoor Asylum continued with a new chief psychiatrist and stricter drug control system. A flawless investigation, Detective.

endings.title: Great Work, Detective!
endings.description: You found the right person and built a strong case!
endings.epilogueText: Dr. Evelyn Marsh was arrested and sentenced to 15 years in prison. Great work, Detective! Here is the true story: Evelyn had been prescribing excessive Thorazine doses to Blackmoor patients for years, selling the surplus on the black market for additional income. Dr. Pemberton noticed the numbers didn't add up during a routine drug audit and began questioning Evelyn. Pemberton was going to report to management the next day. Evelyn acted that night. Taking advantage of the gap in the cleaning schedule, she entered Pemberton's room and administered the lethal dose from her stockpiled Thorazine. Had you gathered a few more critical pieces of evidence, the prescription fraud would have been fully proven and a life sentence could have been achieved. But you accomplished the most important thing: Pemberton's killer was brought to justice!

endings.title: Weak Case
endings.description: You found the right name but the evidence is insufficient.
endings.epilogueText: Evelyn Marsh was detained but her lawyer demonstrated insufficient evidence. She received a 5-year sentence, and with good behavior reduction, was released in 3 years. The prescription fraud case was closed. Blackmoor was spared a scandal but justice was not fully served.

endings.title: The Wrong Professor
endings.description: You accused Dr. Marcus Whitfield. He had unethical research but he wasn't the killer.
endings.epilogueText: Marcus Whitfield was detained. His unethical research was exposed and his license was revoked. But he had no involvement in Pemberton's death. The real killer, Evelyn Marsh, quietly left the hospital while attention was diverted and fled the country. The right crime for the wrong person, Detective.

endings.title: Rahibenin Masumiyeti
endings.description: You accused Sister Agnes. She had a 30-year secret but she wasn't the killer.
endings.epilogueText: Agnes Blackwood was detained. The 1994 incident was reinvestigated but Agnes's involvement could not be proven. Agnes was just a witness, someone who carried the secret. She had a motive to kill Pemberton but didn't act on it. The real killer went free.

endings.title: The Gardener's Grief
endings.description: You accused Thomas Hargrove. He wanted revenge for his sister but he wasn't the killer.
endings.epilogueText: Thomas Hargrove was detained. His sister's story was heartbreaking, but Thomas had decided to take his revenge differently: he was collecting documents to expose Pemberton. He wasn't a killer, he was a brother seeking justice. The real killer was never caught.

endings.title: The Nurse's Tears
endings.description: You accused Catherine Vale. She had a 20-minute gap but she wasn't the killer.
endings.epilogueText: Catherine Vale was detained. When the 20-minute log gap was questioned, the truth came out: Catherine really had been attending to Patient #47's seizure. Hospital records and other nurses' statements confirmed this. Catherine was innocent, just in the wrong place at the wrong time.

endings.title: The Patient's Ordeal
endings.description: You accused Victor Ashbourne. He couldn't leave his cell.
endings.epilogueText: Victor Ashbourne laughed when accused. 'How would I get out of my cell?' His ankle monitor and cell door records conclusively confirmed Victor was in his cell at the time of the incident. The signal loss was a technical glitch. Victor was innocent, just a patient.

statement1.text: I just went to the restroom, I was back in 4 minutes.

statement2.text: The restroom was closed for maintenance at that time.

result.text: Evelyn says she went to the restroom but it was closed! Where was she for those 4 minutes?

statement1.text: I was in my laboratory at that time, I never went into the corridor.

statement2.text: Marcus was seen in the West Wing corridor at 21:20, slowing down in front of Pemberton's office.

result.text: Marcus claims he was in his lab but was spotted in the corridor!

statement1.text: I was praying in the chapel all evening, I never left.

statement2.text: Agnes was seen in the cafeteria at 20:30. Her whereabouts between 20:30 and 22:05 are unknown.

result.text: Agnes says she was in the chapel but was seen in the cafeteria! Where was she for 1.5 hours?

statement1.text: My prescription dosages are completely within normal limits.

statement2.text: Evelyn prescribed a total of 2400mg excess Thorazine to patients over the last 6 months.

result.text: Evelyn's 'normal dosage' claim collapses she prescribed 2400mg excess Thorazine!

statement1.text: I never entered Pemberton's office on the day of the incident.

statement2.text: Fibers from Pemberton's office carpet were found on Evelyn's clothing.

result.text: Evelyn says she never entered the office but office carpet fibers were found on her clothes!

statement1.text: I've had no contact with Thorazine, I'm just a gardener.

statement2.text: Thorazine residue was detected on the inner surface of Thomas's garden glove.

result.text: Thomas says he had no contact with the drug but Thorazine residue was found in his glove!

suspect_evelyn.unavailableMsg: Dr. Marsh şu anda hasta alıyor. Daha sonra gelin.

suspect_marcus.unavailableMsg: Dr. Whitfield laboratuvardan ayrılmış. Yarın sabah gelin.

suspect_thomas.unavailableMsg: Thomas eve gitti. Yarın sabah bahçede bulabilirsin.

suspect_catherine.unavailableMsg: Catherine gece vardiyasında. Öğleden sonra gel.

evidenceReactions.note: Evelyn temizlik logunu öğrenince kayıtsız kaldı.

evidenceReactions.dialogue: Evelyn kaşları çatıyor. 'Ayakkabımda halı lifi mi? Pemberton'in odasına her gün girerim.'

evidenceReactions.dialogue: Marcus'un yüzü kızarıyor. 'O dosyalar bağlam dışında yorumlanıyor. Her şey etik kurul başkanının bilgisi dahilindeydi.'
Marcus yutkunuyor. 'Richard beni tehdit mi ediyormuş? Bu karşılıklı bir gerilimdi.'
Marcus gözlüklerini çıkarır. 'Tamam, kahve almaya çıktım. 5 dakika sürdü.'
Agnes'in elleri titriyor. 'O mektup... çok eski. Geçmişte kalan geçmişte kalmalı.' Gözleri nemleniyor.
Agnes sakin kalıyor. '30 yıldır buradayım. Acil durumlar için anahtarlarım var.'
Agnes dönüp kalıyor. 'Richard... 1994'ü yazmış mı? O çocuk... o bir kazaydı.' Sesi kısılıyor.
Thomas'in çenesi kasılıyor. 'Emily... Pemberton onu öldürdü, erken taburcu ederek.' Gözleri nemli.
evidenceReactions.note: Thomas kız kardeşinin ölümünden Pemberton'i sorumlu tutuyor.

evidenceReactions.dialogue: Thomas şaşkın görünüyor. 'Eldivenlerimde ilaç mı? Eczanede tamir yaparken bulaşmış olmalı.'
Thomas ellerini ovuyor. 'Bodrum kata indim çünkü aletlerimi almam gerekiyordu.'
Catherine saçlarını düzeltiyor. 'O 20 dakika... Hasta kriz geçirdi. Hayat kurtarıyordum.'
evidenceReactions.note: Catherine kayıt boşluğunu acil durumla açıklıyor.

evidenceReactions.dialogue: Catherine'in yüzü soluyor. 'Thorazine mi? Tanrı'm... Ben her gün o ilacı dağıtıyorum.' Elleri titriyor.
Catherine bakışları yere iniyor. 'Gizli proje... Dr. Pemberton benden bazı verileri derlememi istemişti.'
Victor gülmüyor. 'Thorazine. Benim en yakın arkadaşım. Her gün içiriyorlar bana.'
evidenceReactions.note: Victor Thorazine'le kişisel ilişkisi olduğunu ima ediyor.

evidenceReactions.dialogue: Victor parmaklarını sayar. 'Sinyal kaybı. Duvarlar kalın. Her gece olur.'
evidenceReactions.note: Victor sinyal kaybını teknik aksaklıkla açıklıyor.

evidenceReactions.dialogue: Victor duvara bakar. 'Pemberton benim hakkımda ne yazmış? Ben onun en ilginç vakasıyım, değil mi?'
Victor kaşlarını kaldırır. 'Evelyn'in bana fazla ilaç yazdığını mı söylüyorsunuz?'

newspaperArticles.headline: SHOCK AT BLACKMOOR: DIRECTOR FOUND DEAD
newspaperArticles.subheadline: Dr. Pemberton dies in his office
newspaperArticles.body: The director of London's oldest psychiatric hospital, Blackmoor Asylum, Dr. Richard Pemberton, was found dead in his office. Initial reports cited a heart attack, but the autopsy points to a lethal dose of medication. Police are focusing on six suspects.

newspaperArticles.headline: BLACKMOOR INQUIRY: BROKEN ALIBIS
newspaperArticles.subheadline: The hospital's inner world under scrutiny
newspaperArticles.body: In the Pemberton case, cracks have emerged in every suspect's alibi. Security footage, cleaning logs, and hospital records contradict current statements. Scotland Yard has begun using the phrase 'an inside job.'

newspaperArticles.headline: BLACK MARKET DRUG NETWORK SUSPECTED AT BLACKMOOR
newspaperArticles.body: Blackmoor pharmacy records show thousands of doses of Thorazine beyond prescriptions. A long-running black market network may exist inside the hospital. The link between this network and Pemberton's death is being examined directly.

newspaperArticles.headline: 1994'S BURIED SECRET SURFACES
newspaperArticles.subheadline: An old Blackmoor death back on the agenda
newspaperArticles.body: Archive documents regarding Timothy Walsh's death 30 years ago at Blackmoor are being reviewed. The responsible doctor at the time: Richard Pemberton. The family's objections over the years went unanswered. The new investigation is considering tying this old file to today's case.

newspaperArticles.headline: DETECTIVE IN CONTROL AT BLACKMOOR
newspaperArticles.body: The detective leading the case is reportedly close to unraveling a complex evidentiary chain. Hospital staff statements are being retaken due to inconsistencies.

newspaperArticles.headline: BLACKMOOR INVESTIGATION STALLED
newspaperArticles.body: No concrete progress in the Pemberton murder investigation. The family and public are uneasy. Scotland Yard officials said, 'We will issue a statement soon.'

pressEvents.question: Detective, do you suspect Blackmoor staff?

options.text: Every staff member's statement is being taken; I won't be prejudicial.
Yes, it's someone from inside the hospital. We're close.

pressEvents.question: Is Pemberton's link to the 1994 Walsh case connected to this murder?

options.text: We are considering historical links. Commenting is premature.
The Walsh case is part of the inquiry.
